description: The meta-class: the class of all classes. Every entity that is a type or category is instance_of @class.
All software. Root of the software class hierarchy. Every program that executes on a substrate is a member of this class.
Class of software licenses under which programs and source code are distributed.
Class of organizations and companies involved in software development.
Class of human individuals relevant to software history and development.
Class of data file formats used for storing and exchanging information.
Class of communication protocols specifying rules for data exchange between systems.
Class of technical specifications defining standards and interfaces.
Software that manages hardware resources and provides common services for computer programs.
A formal language used to specify instructions for a computer.
A software application for the composition, editing, formatting, and printing of documents.
A software application for the organization and analysis of data in tabular form.
A software application for creating and manipulating raster images.
A software application for creating and editing vector graphics.
A software application for writing and editing source code.
An integrated development environment providing comprehensive facilities for software development.
A software application for creating and displaying slide-based presentations.
Software that serves HTTP responses to client requests over a network.
Software for organized storage, retrieval, and management of structured data.
Software that enables asynchronous communication between services via queued messages.
Software that stores frequently accessed data in memory to reduce latency.
Software for tracking changes to files and coordinating work among multiple contributors.
Software for scheduling and executing recurring tasks at specified times.
Software that automates the compilation and assembly of source code into executable artifacts.
Software responsible for initializing the operating system and managing system services after boot.
Software that automates building, testing, and integrating code changes in a continuous integration workflow.
A large language model deployed as a conversational interface for general-purpose text interaction.
A software framework for composing and orchestrating autonomous AI agents.
An AI-powered tool that assists developers by generating, completing, or reviewing code.
A machine learning model that generates or manipulates images from textual or visual input.
A software component implementing the Language Server Protocol to provide IDE features for a programming language.
A static analysis tool that identifies errors, style violations, and suspicious constructs in source code.
A tool that automatically reformats source code to conform to a style standard.
A permissive free software license originating at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology.
Version 3 of the GNU General Public License, a copyleft free software license published by the Free Software Foundation in 2007.
Version 2.0 of the Apache License, a permissive free software license published by the Apache Software Foundation in 2004.
A permissive free software license derived from the original BSD license, requiring preservation of copyright notice, conditions, and disclaimer in redistributions.
A license under which the publisher retains exclusive intellectual property rights, restricting use, modification, and redistribution.
A designation indicating that a work has no copyright restrictions and may be used freely by anyone.
American multinational technology corporation headquartered in Redmond, Washington, developing software, consumer electronics, and cloud services.
American multinational technology corporation headquartered in Cupertino, California, designing consumer electronics, software, and online services.
American multinational technology corporation specializing in internet services, search, cloud computing, and software.
American multinational technology corporation that manufactures and markets computer hardware, middleware, and software, and provides hosting and consulting services.
American multinational technology corporation specializing in database software, cloud infrastructure, and enterprise software products.
American non-profit organization that supports the development of the Firefox browser and other open-source internet tools.
American non-profit corporation that supports open-source software projects including the Apache HTTP Server and numerous related projects.
American multinational technology corporation operating social media and communication platforms including Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp.
American artificial intelligence safety company that develops large language models including the Claude family.
American artificial intelligence research organization that develops large language models and AI systems including GPT and ChatGPT.
Free software mass collaboration project announced by Richard Stallman in 1983, producing a complete Unix-compatible software system.
American non-profit technology consortium founded to support the development of the Linux kernel and other open-source software.
Hungarian-American software developer known for leading development of Microsoft Word and Excel and for establishing Hungarian notation.
Finnish-American software engineer who created the Linux kernel and the Git version control system.
American computer scientist who designed and implemented the original Unix operating system and co-created the C and Go programming languages.
American computer scientist who created the C programming language and co-developed the Unix operating system at Bell Labs.
Dutch programmer who created the Python programming language and served as its principal author until 2018.
American computer programmer who created the JavaScript programming language while at Netscape in 1995.
Danish computer scientist who designed and implemented the C++ programming language at Bell Labs beginning in 1979.
American free software activist and programmer who launched the GNU Project and founded the Free Software Foundation.
A general-purpose procedural programming language developed at Bell Labs by Dennis Ritchie between 1972 and 1973.
A general-purpose programming language created by Bjarne Stroustrup as an extension of C with object-oriented and generic programming features.
A general-purpose, object-oriented programming language developed by Microsoft, first released in 2000 as part of the .NET platform.
A high-level, dynamically typed, general-purpose programming language emphasizing code readability, created by Guido van Rossum in 1991.
A class-based, object-oriented programming language designed to have as few implementation dependencies as possible, first released by Sun Microsystems in 1995.
A lightweight, interpreted programming language with first-class functions, created by Brendan Eich at Netscape in 1995.
A strongly typed programming language that builds on JavaScript, developed and maintained by Microsoft, first released in 2012.
A systems programming language focused on memory safety and performance without a garbage collector, first publicly released in 2012.
A statically typed, compiled programming language designed at Google by Robert Griesemer, Rob Pike, and Ken Thompson, first released in 2009.
A family of programming languages with a long history and a distinctive, fully parenthesized prefix notation for syntax, originating in 1960 from John McCarthy's work at MIT.
A family of proprietary graphical operating systems developed and sold by Microsoft, first released in 1985.
A Unix-based graphical operating system developed and marketed by Apple Inc., first released in 2001 as Mac OS X.
A family of open-source Unix-like operating systems based on the Linux kernel, first released by Linus Torvalds on September 17, 1991.
A family of multitasking, multiuser operating systems that derive from the original Unix system developed at Bell Labs beginning in 1969.
A Unix derivative originally developed at the University of California, Berkeley, first released in 1978.
A mobile operating system developed and marketed by Apple Inc., first released in 2007 for the iPhone.
A mobile operating system based on a modified version of the Linux kernel, developed by Google and the Open Handset Alliance, first released in 2008.
A file format for word-processing documents based on the Office Open XML standard, developed by Microsoft and first standardized in 2006.
A file format developed by Adobe in 1993 to present documents including text formatting and images in a device-independent manner.
The standard markup language for documents designed to be displayed in a web browser, first publicly described by Tim Berners-Lee in 1991.
A lightweight, text-based data interchange format derived from JavaScript object syntax, first specified in the early 2000s.
A markup language that defines a set of rules for encoding documents in a format that is both human-readable and machine-readable, standardized by the W3C in 1998.
A human-readable data serialization language, first proposed in 2001, often used for configuration files and data exchange.
A raster graphics file format that supports lossless data compression, standardized by the W3C in 1996.
An XML-based vector image format for two-dimensional graphics, standardized by the W3C in 2001.
Mail transfer agent originating at UC Berkeley, the dominant Unix MTA of the 1980s–1990s. Notorious for its labyrinthine configuration syntax (sendmail.cf). Still in production use.
American computer scientist who co-founded WordPerfect Corporation and co-created the WordPerfect word processor.
American software developer who co-created WordPerfect and co-founded WordPerfect Corporation with Alan Ashton.
American software engineer who, along with his brother John Knoll, created Adobe Photoshop in 1987–1988.
Dutch software developer who created the Vim text editor in 1991, maintaining it until his death in 2023.
American software developer who co-created GIMP with Peter Mattis while at the University of California, Berkeley.
American software developer who co-created GIMP with Spencer Kimball while at the University of California, Berkeley.
A word-processing application developed by Microsoft, first released in 1983 and one of the most widely used document-creation programs in the world.
A word-processing application originally created by Alan Ashton and Bruce Bastian at SSI, dominant in the word-processor market during the 1980s, now maintained by Corel.
A web-based word processor offered by Google as part of Google Workspace, launched publicly in 2006.
American software company that develops the Cursor AI code editor.
Open-source AI code assistant company behind the Continue IDE plugin.
American AI company that developed Devin, an autonomous AI software engineer.
Software engineer and creator of the Aider AI pair-programming tool.
A family of large language models developed by Anthropic, first released in March 2023, designed for safety-focused conversational AI and complex reasoning tasks.
An AI chatbot developed by OpenAI, built on the GPT family of large language models, first released in November 2022 as a conversational text generation service.
A family of open-weight large language models released by Meta AI beginning in February 2023, available for research and commercial use under open licenses.
American computer scientist who rewrote the cron daemon for BSD in 1987, producing the widely-deployed Vixie cron.
German software engineer who created PulseAudio and co-created systemd while working at Red Hat.
American computer scientist who created the Make build tool at Bell Labs in 1976.
Japanese software developer who created the Hudson CI server (later renamed Jenkins) while working at Sun Microsystems.
American software development platform and hosting service for version control using Git, acquired by Microsoft in 2018.
Version 2 of the GNU General Public License, a copyleft free software license published by the Free Software Foundation in 1991.

labels.en: GNU Lesser General Public License v2.1

description: Version 2.1 of the GNU Lesser General Public License, a permissive copyleft free software license allowing linking from proprietary software, published by the FSF in 1999.
A permissive free software license requiring only preservation of the copyright notice and disclaimer in redistributions, without the non-endorsement clause of the BSD 3-Clause.
A source-available software license created by MongoDB Inc. in 2018 that requires any service offering the software's functionality to release all related source code.
A permissive free software license used by the PostgreSQL project, similar in structure to the BSD and MIT licenses.
American software company that develops the MongoDB document-oriented database and provides cloud database services under the Atlas brand.
The volunteer and corporate contributor community responsible for the development and maintenance of the PostgreSQL open-source relational database system.
Dutch-American enterprise software company that develops the Elasticsearch search engine and the Elastic Stack (ELK Stack) suite.
Russian software engineer who created the nginx web server while working at Rambler, the Russian internet company, releasing it publicly in 2004.
Italian software engineer known for creating Redis, the in-memory data structure store, while working at a startup in Sicily in 2009.
American computer scientist who designed and implemented SQLite, the most widely deployed relational database engine in the world.
Finnish software engineer who created MySQL and later MariaDB; the latter named after his daughter, as MySQL was named after his daughter My.
American software engineer who created the Caddy web server, known for its automatic HTTPS via Let's Encrypt.
American software engineer who created Mercurial, a distributed version control system, as an alternative to BitKeeper for Linux kernel development in 2005.
A multimodal AI assistant developed by Google DeepMind, formerly known as Bard, launched publicly in March 2023 and rebranded to Gemini in February 2024.
A family of open-weight large language models developed by Mistral AI, a French AI company, first released in September 2023.
An AI-powered code completion tool developed by GitHub and OpenAI, first released in June 2021, that suggests code and entire functions in real time.
A free and open-source cross-platform web server software developed and maintained by the Apache Software Foundation; the most widely used web server in the world from 1996 to the mid-2010s.
A free, open-source, high-performance HTTP server, reverse proxy, and IMAP/POP3 proxy server created by Igor Sysoev and first publicly released in 2004.
An open-source web server written in Go, notable for automatic HTTPS provisioning via Let's Encrypt by default, created by Matt Holt and first released in 2015.
French artificial intelligence company founded in 2023 that develops open-weight large language models.
A time-based job scheduler in Unix-like operating systems, first created by Ken Thompson at Bell Labs and rewritten by Paul Vixie in 1987 to become the dominant implementation.
A suite of software providing an init system, service manager, and system logging for Linux, developed primarily by Lennart Poettering and Kay Sievers, first released in 2010.
A build automation tool that automatically builds executable programs and libraries from source code by reading files called Makefiles, originally created by Stuart Feldman at Bell Labs in 1976.
A powerful, open-source object-relational database management system emphasizing extensibility and SQL compliance, developed by the PostgreSQL Global Development Group.
An open-source relational database management system widely used as a component of web applications, originally created by Michael Widenius and first released in 1995.
A C-language library implementing a small, fast, self-contained, serverless, zero-configuration, transactional SQL database engine, created by D. Richard Hipp in 2000.
An open-source framework for building applications powered by large language models, enabling chaining of LLM calls with tools, agents, and memory, first released in October 2022 by Harrison Chase.
An open-source autonomous AI agent built on GPT-4, developed by Toran Bruce Richards and released in March 2023, one of the earliest examples of an LLM-based autonomous agent.
Co-founder and CEO of LangChain Inc., who created the LangChain framework for building LLM-powered applications.
British software developer who created the Auto-GPT autonomous AI agent in 2023.
A cross-platform build system generator that produces native build files for a target environment from a high-level description in CMakeLists.txt files, first released in 2000.
An open-source build and test tool developed by Google, using a Python-like language (Starlark) for build descriptions, designed for multi-language monorepo builds with reproducible outputs.
A small, speed-focused build system designed to run builds as fast as possible, created by Evan Martin at Google in 2012 and commonly used as a backend for CMake.
American software research and development company specializing in open-source scientific computing tools, and the primary developer of CMake.
A free and open-source word processor included in the LibreOffice office suite, derived from OpenOffice.org Writer and first released in 2011.
A spreadsheet application developed by Microsoft, first released in 1985 for the Macintosh, featuring grid-based calculation, graphing tools, and a macro programming language.
A web-based spreadsheet application offered by Google as part of Google Workspace, launched in 2006.
A raster graphics editor developed by Adobe, originally created by Thomas and John Knoll in 1988 and first commercially released in 1990, widely used in professional image editing.
A free and open-source raster graphics editor, created by Spencer Kimball and Peter Mattis at UC Berkeley in 1995, used for tasks including photo retouching and image editing.
A free, open-source code editor developed by Microsoft, first released in 2015 and built on the Electron framework, supporting extensions for hundreds of programming languages.
A free, open-source, highly configurable text editor built to make creating and changing text efficient, created by Bram Moolenaar in 1991 as an extended version of the vi editor.
A family of highly extensible text editors, the most popular being GNU Emacs, originally created by Richard Stallman at MIT in 1976 and developed under the GNU Project.
A proprietary cross-platform text editor for code, markup, and prose, created by Jon Skinner and first released in 2008, known for its speed and multiple selection feature.
A dependency-based init system for Unix-like operating systems, originally developed by Roy Marples for Gentoo Linux and now maintained as a cross-distribution project.

An init system and service management framework for macOS and other Apple operating systems, developed by Apple and replacing older Unix-style init and cron systems starting with Mac OS X Tiger in 2005.

An open-source automation server used for continuous integration and continuous delivery, created by Kohsuke Kawaguchi as Hudson at Sun Microsystems and renamed Jenkins in 2011 after Oracle acquired Sun.
A cloud-based CI/CD service integrated into GitHub, allowing developers to automate workflows triggered by repository events using YAML-defined action files, launched in 2019.
A hosted continuous integration service used to build and test software projects hosted on GitHub and other version control systems, launched in 2011.
An AI-powered code editor built on Visual Studio Code's codebase, developed by Anysphere, featuring deep LLM integration for code generation, editing, and chat.

An open-source AI pair-programming tool that runs in the terminal and connects to LLMs such as GPT-4 and Claude to assist with coding tasks directly in a local git repository, created by Paul Gauthier.
An agentic coding tool developed by Anthropic that integrates with the terminal and assists developers with understanding and modifying codebases through a conversational interface powered by Claude.
An open-source AI code assistant that integrates with VS Code and JetBrains IDEs, allowing developers to connect their own LLMs for code completion, editing, and chat within the IDE.
A source-available, cross-platform, document-oriented database program that uses JSON-like documents with optional schemas, developed by MongoDB, Inc. and first released in 2009.
An open-source, in-memory data structure store used as a database, cache, message broker, and streaming engine, created by Salvatore Sanfilippo and first released in 2009.
A free and open-source distributed version control system designed to handle everything from small to very large projects with speed and efficiency, created by Linus Torvalds in 2005.
A free, distributed source control management tool, created by Matt Mackall in 2005 as an alternative to BitKeeper for the Linux kernel, emphasizing speed and scalability.
A community-developed, open-source relational database management system forked from MySQL in 2010 by Michael Widenius after Oracle's acquisition of Sun Microsystems.
A search and analytics engine based on Apache Lucene, developed by Elastic N.V. and first released in 2010, supporting distributed, full-text search with a RESTful API.
